title: Multidirectional Equipment, Liquids, and Powders
narrationScript: A crack only shows when the field crosses it, and on a production part cracks come in every orientation. The classic answer is two separate inspections, circular then longitudinal, with the bath and the viewing done twice. Multidirectional equipment exists to collapse that into one operation. This lesson covers how those machines work and what they demand from you, and then we move to the materials themselves, the liquids and powders that actually form your indications. A multidirectional unit combines two or more magnetizing circuits in one machine. A head shot or central conductor circuit drives a circular field. A built in coil drives a longitudinal field. The trick is in the switching. The machine alternates between circuits many times each second, so fast that as the bath flows over the part, particles are being gathered by leakage fields in every direction almost at once. One bath application, one viewing, full orientation coverage. That speed comes with one hard rule. Multidirectional magnetization only works with the continuous method. A part cannot hold two field directions as a residual field. Whatever fires last wins, and the earlier direction is gone. So the particles must be on the part and mobile while the fields are alternating. If anyone ever proposes multidirectional work by the residual method, something is wrong. The second demand is balance. Each direction must be strong enough to hold particles on its own, and neither may drown the other. You cannot prove balance from the ammeter alone, and formulas will not capture a complex forging. You prove it with artificial flaw shims, the quantitative quality indicators, taped to the part in at least two orientations at several locations, including the hardest corners. Adjust each circuit independently until every notch shows crisply, then lock the settings. Where do you meet these machines? High volume production of complex shapes. Crankshafts, steering knuckles, gear blanks, axle forgings. Anywhere two separate magnetizations would double the cycle time on thousands of parts. Now the materials. A wet bath is just particles riding in a liquid vehicle, and the vehicle has a job description. It must be thin, low viscosity, so particles can swim to a leakage field fast. It must wet the whole surface without beading. It must not attack the part. And it must not glow, because a vehicle with its own fluorescence washes out the very indications you are hunting. Oil vehicles are light petroleum distillates chosen for low viscosity, low odor, low fluorescence, and a reasonably high flash point. Water vehicles need help from chemistry. Wetting agents so the water spreads instead of beading. Corrosion inhibitors to protect the parts and the tank. Antifoam agents so the pump does not whip the bath into suds. Safety rides along with both. Oil baths can burn, so flash point matters and arcing at the contacts is taken seriously. Water and electricity share the same bench, so grounding and equipment condition matter. Either liquid dries and irritates skin with repeated contact, so gloves and ventilation are standing practice. Temperature gets a vote too. Wet baths generally live below about one hundred thirty five degrees Fahrenheit. Hotter than that and you cook off vehicle, sometimes violently with water. Hot work belongs to dry powder, with some powders rated to around six hundred degrees Fahrenheit. Cold matters in the other direction. A cold bath thickens, particles slow down, and a water bath in a field truck can freeze solid. The particles themselves are finely milled magnetic irons and iron oxides, sized and shaped on purpose. Visible particles are pigmented red, black, or gray for contrast. Fluorescent particles carry a resin bonded dye that turns ultraviolet light into bright yellow green. Pastes are the older concentrate form, blended into oil baths. Modern dry concentrates for water carry their own wetting and conditioning agents. Mixing is simple and unforgiving. Clean tank. Fill with vehicle to the operating level. Add the measured amount of concentrate, never a guess, with the agitation running. Recirculate until fully dispersed, commonly at least thirty minutes, then verify before any part sees the bath. The check is the settling test. Draw one hundred milliliters from the delivery hose into a pear shaped centrifuge tube, let it settle for thirty minutes, and read the particle volume in the stem. Typical procedure ranges run about zero point one to zero point four milliliter for fluorescent baths, and about one point two to two point four milliliters for visible baths. A lean bath misses fine cracks. A rich bath buries them in background. And watch the tube for a second layer sitting on top of the particles. That band is contamination, lint and dirt and stray oil, and when it grows large, or when the liquid itself starts to fluoresce, the bath is finished and gets replaced.
imagePrompt: Under deep violet UV light in a darkened booth, an inspector raises a pear-shaped settling tube of glowing green fluorescent bath beside a production bench where a forged steering knuckle sits clamped between contact heads.

sections.heading: One Operation, Every Direction
sections.body:
Full MT coverage needs flux roughly perpendicular to every possible flaw — which classically means two complete inspections: a circular shot with bath and viewing, then a longitudinal shot with bath and viewing. On a production line, that doubles everything.

**Multidirectional equipment** collapses the two into one operation. The machine contains two (or more) independent magnetizing circuits sharing the part:

- a **head-shot or central-conductor circuit** producing circular magnetization, and
- a **coil (or cable) circuit** producing longitudinal magnetization.

A high-speed switching network alternates the circuits **many times per second**. While the bath is applied and draining, the part experiences field first in one direction, then the other, over and over — fast enough that leakage fields in **every orientation** capture particles within a single bath-and-view cycle.

Capabilities, in short: full-orientation coverage, one bath application, one viewing, dramatically shorter cycle time, and shot-to-shot consistency that manual two-step processing cannot match. What it is **not**: more penetrating, more tolerant of poor bath condition, or self-verifying. A multidirectional unit is two magnetizing systems sharing one part — and both still obey every rule you already know about field strength and direction.

sections.caption: A multidirectional bench alternates a circular-field circuit and a longitudinal-field coil circuit many times per second while the bath drains, with QQI shims proving balanced coverage in both directions.

sections.heading: Sequencing and the Continuous-Method Rule
sections.body:
The sequencing is everything. Each circuit fires in rapid alternation — circular, longitudinal, circular, longitudinal — with the pattern repeating throughout the bath-drain window. Two consequences follow.

First, **multidirectional magnetization is continuous-method only.** A part cannot retain two field directions at once; whichever circuit fired last sets the residual field and erases the other direction. Magnetize first and bathe afterward (the residual method) and you would have coverage in exactly one direction — the last one. Particles must be present and mobile **while the fields alternate**, so bath timing and shot timing are locked together by the machine's sequencer.

Second, **each direction must do its full job in its share of the cycle.** The alternation must be fast enough that indications held by one direction are not washed off or released before that direction returns. Bath valve timing, drain interval, and shot-train length are qualified procedure settings on these machines — never casual adjustments.

Watch the failure mode: if one circuit is disabled, mistimed, or starved of current while the line keeps running, the machine still looks busy — and an entire family of flaw orientations goes undetected.

sections.heading: Balance, Controls, and Applications
sections.body:
**Control and operation factors.** Each circuit has independent current control, and the central setup task is **balance**: every direction strong enough to hold particles, no direction so strong it dominates. You cannot prove balance from ammeters or formulas — complex geometry steers flux in ways calculation misses. The accepted proof is **QQI artificial-flaw shims** fastened to the part in at least two orientations at multiple locations, including the worst corners. Adjust each circuit until every notch indicates crisply, then lock the settings into the technique. Re-verify whenever the part number, fixturing, or settings change. A Hall-effect gaussmeter helps quantify the tangential surface field — procedures commonly look for values on the order of 30–60 G — but shims demonstrate the thing that matters: indications actually forming in every direction.

**Applications.** Multidirectional machines earn their cost on **high-volume, complex-geometry parts**: crankshafts, steering knuckles and suspension forgings, gear blanks, couplings, axle and spindle forgings — parts with bores, flanges, and section changes where flaws arrive in every orientation, and where doubling the cycle for two separate magnetizations would choke production. For one-off work, simple shapes, or field jobs, conventional sequential shots remain the practical choice.

sections.heading: The Liquid Vehicle: Requirements and Temperature

sections.title: Bath chemistry deserves PPE
sections.body: **Oil baths burn** — keep flash point per the specification, keep arcs away (clamp before the shot, never break contact under current), and treat oil mist as the ignition hazard it is. **Water baths conduct** — water plus thousands of amperes means grounded equipment, maintained insulation, and dry gloves. Either vehicle de-fats and irritates skin with repeated contact: gloves, aprons, and ventilation are standing practice. Fluorescent work adds the UV-A eye and skin practices covered in the lighting lesson.

sections.heading: Powders, Pastes, and Mixing
sections.body:
**What is in the can.** MT particles are finely divided magnetic irons and iron oxides, milled to controlled size and shape distributions — wet-method particles far finer than dry powders, fine enough to ride a liquid film into tight leakage fields. Visible particles carry pigment (black, red, gray) chosen for contrast against the surface; fluorescent particles are bonded by resin to a dye that converts UV-A into bright yellow-green light. **Pastes** are the older concentrate form — particles pre-blended with binders for stirring into oil baths. Modern **dry concentrates** for water baths build in the wetting agents, inhibitors, and antifoam, so the mix needs only measurement and dispersion.

**Mixing procedure** (typical — the manufacturer's data sheet and your written procedure govern):

1. Start with a **clean tank and pump**; old sludge contaminates a fresh bath immediately.
2. Fill with vehicle to the normal operating level.
3. **Measure** the concentrate for that volume — weighed or measured, never estimated.
4. Add it slowly **with agitation running**, so particles disperse instead of clumping.
5. **Recirculate until fully dispersed** — commonly at least 30 minutes — then run a settling test before the bath touches production parts.

Sample from the delivery side (hose or nozzle) with the bath agitated — never dipped quietly off the top of the tank.

sections.caption: The settling test: 100 mL drawn from the agitated bath settles for 30 minutes in a pear-shaped centrifuge tube, with the particle column read in the graduated stem and any contamination band judged separately.

sections.heading: Proportions and Bath Strength
sections.body:
**Why proportions are exact.** Concentration is sensitivity. A **lean** bath cannot deliver enough particles to a weak leakage field — fine cracks never build a visible indication. A **rich** bath buries the part in background: fluorescent backgrounds glow, visible baths streak, and real indications lose contrast. You cannot judge concentration by eye; you measure it.

The instrument is the **settling test**: agitate the bath (commonly 30 minutes or more), draw **100 mL** from the delivery hose into a pear-shaped centrifuge tube, let it settle — commonly **30 minutes** — and read the settled particle volume in the graduated stem. Typical procedure ranges: **0.1–0.4 mL per 100 mL for fluorescent** baths and **1.2–2.4 mL per 100 mL for visible** baths. Low reading → add measured concentrate; high reading → add vehicle; re-agitate and re-test either way.

**Bath strength and quality drift constantly.** Drag-out on parts leans the bath; carrier evaporation richens it; pump shear slowly grinds particles and dulls fluorescent brilliance. The settling tube also reads **quality**: a separate band above the particles is contamination — lint, dirt, oil in a water bath — and a carrier that fluoresces under UV-A is breaking down. Stratified layers, contamination approaching roughly 30 % of the particle column, or a glowing carrier mean the bath gets **replaced, not nursed**. A Ketos-type ring or QQI check confirms system performance after any adjustment.

figures.title: Wet-bath settling test and acceptance ranges

interactive.title: Bringing a Multidirectional Bench Online
interactive.intro: You are setting up a multidirectional wet bench to inspect forged steering knuckles. The unit has two circuits — a head-shot circuit for circular magnetization and a coil circuit for longitudinal. Production starts in an hour, and the shift lead wants parts moving.

steps.prompt: Before the first production part runs, how do you prove the multidirectional setup actually covers every flaw orientation?
options: Read the ammeter for each circuit and confirm both match the calculated values
Fasten QQI artificial-flaw shims in at least two orientations at several locations — including the worst corners — and confirm crisp indications in every direction
Run a pie gauge once at the center of the part
steps.feedback: Ammeters and formulas cannot prove balanced coverage on complex geometry — flux takes paths calculation misses. QQIs in multiple orientations at multiple locations are the accepted demonstration; a single pie-gauge reading shows rough field direction at one spot, not system balance.

steps.prompt: The shims show crisp indications from the head-shot (circular) field, but the notches that depend on the coil (longitudinal) field are faint. Your move:
options: Raise the coil circuit's current independently and re-verify with the shims, leaving the head-shot setting alone
Raise both circuits equally to be safe
Slow the bath flow so particles linger longer
steps.feedback: Multidirectional circuits adjust independently — that is the point of the controls. Raising both preserves the imbalance and risks over-magnetization and furring from the already-strong circuit. Bath flow is not the deficiency; field strength in one direction is.

steps.prompt: A new technician asks why this machine cannot run the residual method to save bath. The correct answer:
options: Residual would work but takes slightly longer
The part retains only the last field direction applied — earlier directions vanish — so particles must be present and mobile while the fields alternate. Multidirectional is continuous-method only
Residual is prohibited with water-based baths specifically
steps.feedback: A part cannot store two field directions; the last shot wins. Bathe after magnetizing and you have single-direction coverage at best. That is why the sequencer ties bath application and the alternating shot train together.

steps.prompt: Final pre-shift check: the fluorescent bath settling test reads 0.05 mL per 100 mL against a procedure range of 0.1–0.4 mL. Production is waiting. What now?
options: Run anyway — a lean bath just means cleaner, lower-background viewing
Add measured concentrate per the manufacturer's data, recirculate until dispersed, and re-test before any part runs
Double the magnetizing current on both circuits to compensate for fewer particles
steps.feedback: A lean bath misses fine cracks — there are simply not enough particles to build an indication, and no amperage can substitute for them. Adjust the concentration, re-disperse, re-test, and only then run parts. Production pressure never overrides bath strength.

checkQuestions.question: Multidirectional magnetization is valid only with the continuous method because:
options: Residual fields are too strong for wet baths
A part retains only the last field direction applied, so earlier directions are lost unless particles are present while the fields alternate
The continuous method uses less current per circuit
Residual methods work only with dry powder
checkQuestions.explanation: A part cannot hold two residual field directions — the last shot erases the others. Coverage in all orientations exists only while the circuits are alternating, so particles must be on the part and mobile at that moment.

checkQuestions.question: The accepted way to prove a multidirectional setup delivers balanced coverage in all flaw orientations is:
options: Confirming both ammeters match the calculated amperages
QQI artificial-flaw shims applied in at least two orientations at several part locations
A single pie-gauge reading at the center of the part
Lifting a 40 lb weight with each circuit energized
checkQuestions.explanation: Complex geometry steers flux in ways formulas and meters cannot capture. Shims with artificial flaws in multiple orientations and locations demonstrate that every direction actually forms indications — the lifting check applies to yokes, not benches.

checkQuestions.question: During a multidirectional run, the head-shot (circular) circuit drifts well below its qualified amperage while the coil circuit stays normal. The flaws most at risk of being missed are:
options: Transverse cracks across the part axis
Longitudinal cracks and seams running parallel to the part axis
Subsurface flaws only
None — the coil circuit compensates for the weak circuit
checkQuestions.explanation: The circular field is what crosses axis-parallel (longitudinal) flaws at a high angle. Lose it and those flaws lose their leakage fields — the longitudinal field from the coil runs nearly parallel to them and cannot compensate.

checkQuestions.question: Which property set describes a good oil vehicle for a wet MT bath?
options: High viscosity, strong natural fluorescence, low flash point
Low viscosity, minimal inherent fluorescence, relatively high flash point
High viscosity, so particles never settle in the tank
Any clean fuel oil drawn from shop stock
checkQuestions.explanation: Particles must migrate fast (low viscosity, around 3 cSt at 100 °F typical), the carrier must not glow under UV-A and mask indications, and flash point — commonly specified near 200 °F or higher — controls the fire hazard.

checkQuestions.question: A 100 mL settling test on a fluorescent bath reads 0.7 mL after 30 minutes against a procedure range of 0.1–0.4 mL. The correct response is:
options: Run parts — more particles always mean more sensitivity
Add more measured concentrate to stabilize the reading
Add carrier vehicle to dilute into range, re-agitate, and re-test
Shorten future settling times to 10 minutes so the readings drop
checkQuestions.explanation: 0.7 mL is over-rich: excess particles raise fluorescent background and bury real indications. The fix is measured dilution with vehicle, full re-dispersion, and a repeat settling test — never gaming the test itself.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §4.5 (liquids and powders)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V Article 7 cover bath preparation, concentration, and verification (generic reference)
Typical settling-test ranges: fluorescent ≈ 0.1–0.4 mL, visible ≈ 1.2–2.4 mL per 100 mL bath
